verdict: All three LinkedIn posts and the newsletter meet the depth and specificity bar. The Curse of Knowledge framing is precise and well-applied to VRM dynamics. Post 3 and the newsletter have the strongest operational depth. No shallow work in this set.
strengths: Post 1 correctly identifies the Curse of Knowledge as a communication design problem, not a knowledge deficit, and grounds it in a specific appointment scenario
Post 2 uses effective contrast structure (abstract vs. concrete) with specific, usable examples the reader can directly apply
Post 3 introduces the four-part story structure with a clear distinction between testimonial and story as different types of evidence
Newsletter genuinely extends the posts rather than repeating them: covers Simple, Unexpected, and Emotional principles not anchored by individual posts
Owner type differentiation in the newsletter (Investor, Steward, Skeptic) is specific and correctly mapped to the SUCCESs framework
Scripts throughout are plausible operator language, not sales training manual language
All posts use the correct header format, Post X of Y line, all-caps title, and CTA footer
problems: Post 1 ending is slightly soft: 'will outperform the operator who brings the full playbook every time' is a mild claim that could land harder
required_fixes: Sharpen the closing line of Post 1 to land with more conviction rather than a comparative claim
